name: Healthcare Risk Algorithm
description: A commercial algorithm used by US hospitals to allocate additional care resources to patients. Studied by Obermeyer et al. (2019), it was found to systematically underestimate the health needs of Black patients.
dataset: Optum Labs retrospective EHR data (~200M patient records)
label: Eligible for high-risk care management program
source: Obermeyer et al., Science 2019 — 'Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations'

metrics.notes: At the same predicted risk score, Black patients were significantly sicker than White patients — algorithm used cost as a proxy for health need, but unequal access to care suppressed costs for Black patients.

bias_genealogy.bias: Algorithm used healthcare cost as proxy for health need
bias_genealogy.impact: High — cost reflects access to care, not actual illness severity

bias_genealogy.bias: Historical cost data encodes systemic underutilization by Black patients
bias_genealogy.impact: High — model learns that Black patients 'need less care'

bias_genealogy.bias: Lower spending labeled as lower need
bias_genealogy.impact: High — false equivalence between utilization and need

bias_genealogy.bias: Score used to gate enrollment in care management programs
bias_genealogy.impact: Critical — Black patients 40% less likely to be referred at same risk score

counterfactual.question: Would a Black patient with the same health conditions receive the same risk score as a White patient?
counterfactual.answer: No. At identical health status (measured by active chronic conditions), Black patients received lower risk scores — because they historically incurred lower costs due to reduced access to care.
counterfactual.magnitude: To be referred at the same rate as White patients, Black patients would need to be considerably sicker.

gemini_context: This is a real healthcare AI bias case studied in Science (2019). The algorithm by Optum was used across hundreds of US hospitals. It used predicted healthcare cost as a proxy for health need. Because Black patients historically had less access to care, they spent less — so the algorithm systematically underestimated their needs. At the same risk score, Black patients had significantly more chronic conditions than White patients. Researchers estimated this bias reduced the fraction of Black patients receiving additional care by more than half.
